title: Core backend architecture principles
summary: Backend architecture keeps policy, use-case orchestration, interface adapters, and infrastructure details separate so dependency direction remains inward.

title: Dependency inversion and repository boundary
summary: Repository abstractions are a concrete way to invert persistence dependencies while keeping infrastructure implementations outside policy code.

title: Service and use-case boundary
summary: Application services coordinate use cases through domain policy and ports while avoiding transport and database mechanics.

title: Structure and behavior workflow
summary: Structural refactors and behavior changes should remain distinguishable when mixing them would raise review, rollback, or reasoning cost.

title: Coupling, cohesion, and change cost
summary: Coupling increases change cost, decoupling must be economically justified, and cohesion guides where boundaries should be placed.

title: Reversible architecture changes
summary: Under uncertainty, prefer small reversible structure changes that preserve options and reduce regret before broad rewrites.

title: FastAPI framework adapter
summary: FastAPI routers map to interface adapters, response models map to DTO/schema boundaries, and SQLAlchemy belongs behind infrastructure ports.

title: FastAPI Depends layer leak risk
summary: FastAPI Depends is useful for dependency wiring, but using it inside application services can leak framework APIs across architecture boundaries.

title: Spring framework adapter
summary: Spring controllers map to interface adapters, services map to application coordination, repositories map to persistence boundaries, and JPA belongs in infrastructure.

title: Spring API contract and security boundaries
summary: OpenAPI generated contracts shape DTO/schema boundaries, while Spring Security filters, JWT, OAuth2, roles, CORS, and CSRF stay at security/interface boundaries.

title: Spring reactive boundary
summary: WebFlux, Reactive Streams, Mono, Flux, and R2DBC support non-blocking adapters, but reactive framework types should be contained unless the architecture intentionally exposes them.
